prompt_text: Best MLOps platforms for track machine learning experiments and compare model performance across teams
response:
For **tracking machine learning experiments** and **comparing model performance across teams**, the strongest widely used options are **Weights & Biases (W&B)**, **Comet ML**, **MLflow**, and **DVC**; if you want a managed enterprise platform, **Databricks MLflow** and **DataRobot/Valohai** are also common choices.[1][2][4][5][7]

- **Weights & Biases (W&B)**: Best known for experiment tracking, dataset versioning, and model management; it is described as a developer-first MLOps platform, which makes it a strong fit for team collaboration and performance comparison across runs.[5]
- **Comet ML**: Strong for tracking, comparing, and optimizing models, with support for experiment tracking, production monitoring, and integrations with popular frameworks; G2 also highlights its experiment tracking capabilities.[1][2]
- **MLflow**: A common default for teams that want experiment tracking plus a model registry, with low overhead and broad ecosystem support; Databricks specifically recommends it for teams early in their MLOps journey.[2][4]
- **DVC**: Best when you want Git-based dataset versioning and reproducible experiments alongside tracking; it is useful for teams that need disciplined reproducibility more than a full UI-heavy experimentation suite.[2][7]

A practical way to choose:

| Need | Best fit | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Best experiment UI and collaboration | **Weights & Biases** | Strong run comparison, dashboards, and team workflows.[5] |
| Strong all-around experiment tracking | **Comet ML** | Built for tracking, comparing, and optimizing models across projects.[1][2] |
| Open-source baseline with model registry | **MLflow** | Lightweight, widely adopted, and good for standardizing experiments.[2][4] |
| Reproducibility with Git workflows | **DVC** | Good for teams already centered on Git and data/model versioning.[2][7] |

If your main goal is *cross-team model comparison*, **W&B** and **Comet ML** are usually the most directly suited because both emphasize experiment tracking and comparison workflows.[1][5] If you want a more open-source, platform-neutral standard, **MLflow** is the safest default.[2][4]

If you want, I can also rank these by **enterprise readiness**, **ease of adoption**, or **open-source vs. managed**.
